Slowly cooled specimens of the 50 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ material have produced mixtures of two phases.

One of these phases, with refractive indices of $O = 1.508$ and $E = 1.500$ , was previously believed to be $\mathsf{NaZrF}_5$ ; the other material, with refractive indices $a = 1.420$ and $y = 1.432$ , is believed to be $\mathsf{Na}_3\mathsf{Zr}_4\mathsf{F}_{19}^{14}$ .

In addition, a third phase known as R-3 (refractive indices of $O = 1.445$ and $E = 1.417$ ), which is described below, occurs frequently in compositions containing 47 to 57 mole $\%$ $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ .

Samples quenched after heating to a high temperature $(750^{\circ}\mathsf{C})$ followed by equilibration at temperatures near the liquidus show lower liquidus temperatures than those which have never been above the equilibration temperature.
Data from quenching experiments, in agreement with previous thermal data, show the liquidus temperature at 57 mole $\%$ ZrF4 (near the Na3Zr4F19 composition) to be $530^{\circ}C$ .
The primary phase from 52 to 57 mole $\%$ ZrF4 appears to be Na3Zr4F19.

Below the solidus temperature (510°C when approached from above, 519°C when approached from below), NaZr4F19 and the material formerly believed to be NaZrF5 are found over the composition interval 52 to 57 mole $\%$ ZrF4.

However, if $\mathsf{NaZrF}_5$ and $\mathsf{Na}_{3}\mathsf{Zr}_{4}\mathsf{F}_{19}$ coexist below the solidus at 50 mole $\% \mathsf{ZrF}_4$ , then $\mathsf{Na}_{3}\mathsf{Zr}_{4}\mathsf{F}_{19}$ must be the primary phase.
When equilibrium is approached from higher temperatures with 50 mole $\% \mathsf{ZrF}_4$ , only glass or glass with R-3 is found above $511^{\circ}C$ .
Below $511^{\circ}C$ , "NaZrF" and $\mathsf{Na}_{3}\mathsf{Zr}_{4}\mathsf{F}_{19}$ are found.
When equilibrium is approached from below, "NaZrF" and $\mathsf{Na}_{3}\mathsf{Zr}_{4}\mathsf{F}_{19}$ coexist at as high as $519^{\circ}C$ ; above this temperature, glass or glass and R-3 are found.

When equilibrium is approached from higher temperatures with 47 mole $\%$ ZrF, the liquidus temperature is $510^{\circ}C$ ; "NaZrF" and liquid coexist at as high as $523^{\circ}C$ when approached from below.

While other explanations are possible, it appears that the crystalline material of refractive indices $O = 1.508$ and $E = 1.500$ is not $\mathsf{NaZrF}_5$ but $\mathsf{Na}_{9}\mathsf{Zr}_{8}\mathsf{F}_{41}$ ; this material probably melts congruently at $523^{\circ}\mathsf{C}$ and has a eutectic with $\mathsf{Na}_{3}\mathsf{Zr}_{4}\mathsf{F}_{19}$ at about 52 mole % $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ .

The $\mathsf{Na}_{9}\mathsf{Zr}_{8}\mathsf{F}_{41}$ compound forms solid solutions with $\mathsf{Na}_{3}\mathsf{Zr}_{2}\mathsf{F}_{11}$ in which the optical properties vary in a uniform manner with composition.
The unidentified phase R-3 which has appeared over the 45 to 57 mole $\%$ ZrF4 range has almost without exception been associated with a glass phase.
In one case, a sample containing 49.9 mole $\%$ ZrF4 that was quenched from a temperature far above the liquidus was nearly pure R-3.

Samples of this material equilibrated at 485 and $498^{\circ}C$ before quenching yielded crystals too small for petrographic examination; x-ray diffraction indicated that the material had decomposed completely into NaZrF41 and NaZrF19.
It is possible that R-3 is the compound NaZrF5.

It seems obvious that no complex compounds of $\mathsf{ZrF}_4$ content higher than 57 mole $\%$ exist in this system.

Turbulent flow is simulated by suddenly stopping a rotating V-shaped capsule which has been heated to $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ by a gas furnace.

In order to evaluate the relative importance of flow conditions and of temperature, static tests were run at the same time at 600 and at $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , since the welding required in the fabrication of the V-shaped capsules could have changed the properties of the tube walls and thus changed their resistance to corrosion.
In another series of check tests, stationary capsules were thermally cycled by alternately heating and cooling from 650 to $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Tilting-furnace tests were also run in which the hot- and cold-end capsule wall temperatures were kept at 650 and $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , respectively.

These curves show that for the first 200 hr the fine-grained specimen has a much slower creep rate than the coarse-grained specimen, but a transition occurs and a new and faster creep rate is established and maintained until fracture.

It can be seen that up to 700 hr and about $4.5\%$ elongation the fine-grained material will show better creep properties than the coarse-grained material, but after 700 hr, the coarse-grained specimen will be superior.
Figure 7.4 shows rupture curves for two different heats of Inconel tested at $1500^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ in argon.
